ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fruit juice class; no specific 'mixed fruit juice less sugar packaged' subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fruit juices and nectars broadly; no specific less-sugar packaged mixed fruit juice code exists. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged mixed fruit juice less sugar; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one 250 ml carton or bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g, commonly consumed as a snack or with a meal in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Fruit Juice (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mixed Fruit Juice (Less Sugar, Packaged), CR ID D03040017. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 applied based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for mixed/blended fruit juice products. Per the standard GI classification scale used by the University of Sydney and the International GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2008), GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (55 × 10.18 × 2.50) / 100 = 14.0. The 'less sugar' formulation reduces absolute sugar content but does not materially alter GI relative to standard mixed fruit juice. Confidence 0.75 reflecting analogous estimate from approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.86 g/100 g) in a liquid form; despite low sodium (15 mg/100 g), excess sugar intake independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage; despite the 'less sugar' label, the 8.86 g sugar per 100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold and liquid sugars are rapidly absorbed, independently raising cardiovascular and blood pressure risk — replace with plain water or unsweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (8.86 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.15 g/100 g) and low GI (55) results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the 'less sugar' label does not make this beverage suitable for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; the 8.86 g sugar per 100 g combined with negligible fibre (0.15 g/100 g) will cause rapid blood glucose elevation, and per ADA guidelines all sugar-sweetened beverages including reduced-sugar variants are unsuitable for diabetes management — choose water, plain green tea, or genuinely unsweetened drinks instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sugar content (8.86 g/100 g) in liquid form contributes to excess caloric intake, triglyceride elevation, and metabolic risk factors associated with cardiovascular disease; fat content is negligible (0.05 g/100 g) and sodium is low (15 mg/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should minimise consumption of this beverage; while sodium and fat are low, the 8.86 g sugar per 100 g in liquid form promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and excess caloric intake — limit to occasional use and prefer whole fruit or unsweetened beverages to reduce cardiovascular metabolic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (8.86 g/100 g) with negligible fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenols promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; whole fruit is preferred over juice for RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the 8.86 g sugar per 100 g promotes inflammatory mediators and the absence of fibre and intact polyphenols from whole fruit means no anti-inflammatory benefit is conferred — choose whole fruit or unsweetened beverages instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Fruit Juice (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) blended fruit juice beverage sold in packaged form in Singapore, formulated with reduced added sugar compared to standard fruit juice drinks. It is produced by blending various fruit juices — typically combinations of orange, apple, guava, or tropical fruits — with water and a reduced quantity of added sugar, then pasteurised and packaged in cartons or bottles for retail sale. The beverage has a sweet, fruity flavour with a thin, watery texture typical of diluted juice blends. Despite the 'less sugar' marketing claim, this product contains 8.86 g of sugar per 100 g and only 0.15 g of dietary fibre per 100 g, meaning it retains the glycaemic characteristics of a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). The low glycemic index (GI 55, classified as low per University of Sydney scale) and low fibre content result in rapid postprandial glucose absorption. Per 100 g, it provides 43 kcal, 0.4 g protein, 0.05 g fat, and 10.18 g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at 15 mg/100 g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar content, and is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis management. It is plant-based, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Commonly consumed as a snack or accompaniment to meals in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D03040017), an approved primary regional source with confidence 0.95. GI value estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous fruit juice references. Multilingual names provided for all mandatory Singaporean cuisine languages.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a very low-fat beverage with total fat of only 0.05 g/100 g, making saturated fat effectively zero). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific packaged mixed fruit juice less sugar product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03040017 or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source D03040017). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; this is a plant-based fruit juice product containing no animal-derived ingredients). GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fruit juice references; per the University of Sydney and Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low' — gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' accordingly. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 10.18 × 2.50) / 100 = 14.0. The 'less sugar' label indicates reduced added sugar relative to standard variants but the product still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage at 8.86 g sugar/100 g. This record is classified as a Singaporean commercial beverage; full multilingual naming (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) has been applied per G6 rules.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from input JSON. Ontology match types set to closest_parent. Full multilingual naming applied.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Added romanized alias to flat aliases array for q8 consistency.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. Updated health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low GI (55)' rather than 'medium GI'. Updated natural_language_description to state 'low glycemic index (GI 55)'. Updated notes to document the correction.
